The animaOS monorepo uses **Nx** for task orchestration and **Bun** for package management. All projects are auto-discovered via `project.json` files.

## Directory Layout

```
animaos-kit/
├── apps/
│   ├── docs/               # Public user-facing docs (Astro + Starlight)
│   ├── contributor-docs/   # Contributor docs (this site)
│   └── web/                # Web UI
├── hosts/
│   ├── rust-daemon/        # Production Rust host
│   ├── elixir-phoenix/     # Placeholder
│   └── python-service/     # Placeholder
├── packages/
│   ├── core-rust/          # Rust engine crates
│   │   ├── crates/
│   │   │   ├── anima-core/
│   │   │   ├── anima-memory/
│   │   │   └── anima-swarm/
│   ├── core-ts/            # TypeScript contracts
│   ├── sdk/                # TypeScript SDK
│   ├── cli/                # Command-line interface
│   └── ...                 # Other shared packages
├── tools/
│   └── workspace-dev/      # Dev launcher
├── nx.json                 # Nx workspace config
├── package.json            # Root workspace manifest
└── Cargo.toml             # Rust workspace manifest
```

## Dependency Graph

## Package Details

### `packages/core-rust/`

Reusable Rust engine crates. These have **no I/O dependencies** — no HTTP clients, no database drivers, no filesystem access. They operate on pure data structures and trait interfaces.

| Crate | What it does | Key exports |
|-------|-------------|-------------|
| `anima-core` | Agent execution loop, trait interfaces, runtime primitives | `AgentRuntime`, `ModelAdapter`, `Provider`, `Evaluator`, `DatabaseAdapter`, `EngineEvent` |
| `anima-memory` | BM25-backed memory storage, vector search, temporal reasoning | `MemoryManager`, `BM25`, `MemoryRecallResult`, `MemoryEvaluationDecision` |
| `anima-swarm` | Multi-agent coordination strategies | `SwarmCoordinator`, `MessageBus`, `SwarmStrategy` |

See [Rust Core](/architecture/rust-core/) for details.

### `packages/core-ts/`

Shared TypeScript contracts used by the SDK, CLI, TUI, and any TypeScript host. Contains **no runtime dependencies** on HTTP frameworks or databases.

Key modules:
- `types/` — Agent, component, model, plugin, event types
- `runtime/` — `AgentRuntime`, `EventBus`
- `adapters/` — OpenAI, Anthropic, Ollama model adapters
- `helpers/` — Typed identity functions (`agent()`, `action()`, `plugin()`)
- `daemon-health/` — Daemon warning transition helpers

See [TypeScript Core](/architecture/typescript-core/) for details.

### `hosts/rust-daemon/`

The production-ready Rust host. It wraps the core crates in an Axum HTTP/SSE server, providing:
- Provider adapters (OpenAI, Anthropic, Ollama, etc.)
- Memory snapshots (JSON + optional SQLite)
- Optional Postgres step-log durability plus explicit retry-key tool-step reuse
- SSE streaming for swarm events
- Prometheus metrics, readiness probes, OpenAPI docs

See [Rust Daemon](/hosts/rust-daemon/) for details.

### `tools/workspace-dev/`

Manages the dev environment. It reads `nx.json` and starts the selected host plus any dependent processes (web UI, docs, playground).

```bash
# Start the Rust host + web UI + docs
bun dev --host rust

# Start a different host (when implemented)
bun dev --host elixir
```

The supported host keys are `rust`, `elixir`, and `python`. Only `rust` is production-ready today.

<Aside type="note">
  Hosts are intentionally separate from packages. If code is intended to be reused by multiple hosts, it belongs under `packages/`, not `hosts/`.
</Aside>

## Nx Project Discovery

Nx discovers projects by scanning for `project.json` files. To see all projects:

```bash
bun x nx show projects
```

To see a project's targets:

```bash
bun x nx show project @animaOS-SWARM/docs
```

To see the dependency graph:

```bash
bun x nx graph
```

## Adding a New Package

1. Create the directory under `packages/` or `hosts/`
2. Add a `project.json` with `name`, `sourceRoot`, and `targets`
3. Nx will auto-discover it on the next command

See the existing `project.json` files in `apps/docs/` and `hosts/rust-daemon/` for examples.

## Build Order

Nx handles build order via the dependency graph. Key relationships:

- `@animaOS-SWARM/sdk` depends on `@animaOS-SWARM/core`
- `@animaOS-SWARM/cli` depends on `@animaOS-SWARM/sdk`
- `rust-daemon` depends on `anima-core`, `anima-memory`, `anima-swarm`
- `workspace-dev` depends on all app projects

When you run `bun x nx build @animaOS-SWARM/sdk`, Nx automatically builds `@animaOS-SWARM/core` first.

The Rust core is the heart of animaOS. It lives in `packages/core-rust/crates/` and follows a single rule: **core defines behavior, hosts provide reality**.

This means the core has zero I/O dependencies — no HTTP clients, no database drivers, no filesystem access. It operates on pure data structures and trait interfaces. A host (like the Rust Daemon) wraps the core and provides the "real world": network calls to LLM providers, persistence to disk, and HTTP APIs.

## Crate Structure

```
packages/core-rust/
├── Cargo.toml              # Workspace manifest
└── crates/
    ├── anima-core/
    │   ├── src/
    │   │   ├── lib.rs        # Public exports
    │   │   ├── agent.rs      # AgentConfig, AgentState, AgentStatus
    │   │   ├── components.rs # Provider, Evaluator traits
    │   │   ├── events.rs     # EngineEvent, EventType
    │   │   ├── model.rs      # ModelAdapter, ModelGenerateRequest
    │   │   ├── persistence.rs # DatabaseAdapter, Step
    │   │   ├── primitives.rs # Content, Message, TaskResult
    │   │   └── runtime.rs    # AgentRuntime, AgentRuntimeSnapshot
    │   └── Cargo.toml
    ├── anima-memory/
    │   ├── src/
    │   │   ├── lib.rs        # Public exports
    │   │   ├── bm25.rs       # BM25 index, TextAnalyzer
    │   │   ├── memory_manager.rs # MemoryManager, recall, evaluate
    │   │   ├── vector_index.rs   # InMemoryVectorIndex
    │   │   └── eval_harness.rs   # Memory eval test cases
    │   └── Cargo.toml
    └── anima-swarm/
        ├── src/
        │   ├── lib.rs        # Public exports
        │   ├── coordinator.rs # SwarmCoordinator
        │   ├── message_bus.rs # MessageBus
        │   ├── strategies.rs  # Supervisor, RoundRobin, Dynamic
        │   └── types.rs       # SwarmConfig, SwarmState
        └── Cargo.toml
```

## Public API Surface

### `anima-core`

| Module | Key Types |
|--------|-----------|
| `agent` | `AgentConfig`, `AgentSettings`, `AgentState`, `AgentStatus`, `TokenUsage` |
| `components` | `Evaluator`, `EvaluatorResult`, `Provider`, `ProviderResult` |

## Architecture

**Host Layer** — Axum HTTP server, provider adapters, memory snapshot I/O

**Rust Core** — Three crates with zero I/O dependencies:

| Crate | Key Types |
|-------|-----------|
| `anima-core` | `AgentRuntime`, `AgentRuntimeSnapshot`, `ModelAdapter` (trait), `Provider` (trait), `Evaluator` (trait), `EngineEvent` |
| `anima-memory` | `MemoryManager`, `BM25`, vector search, temporal KG, relationship graph |
| `anima-swarm` | `SwarmCoordinator`, `MessageBus`, strategies (supervisor, round-robin, dynamic) |

## Design Rules

1. **Zero I/O** — Core crates never open sockets, files, or database connections
2. **Trait boundaries** — All external concerns are abstracted behind traits (`ModelAdapter`, `DatabaseAdapter`)
3. **Host-owned snapshots** — `anima-memory` supports `snapshot()` / `replace_snapshot()`, and `anima-core` exposes `AgentRuntimeSnapshot` for inspection. The Rust daemon can restore registered agents and swarms from `ANIMAOS_RS_CONTROL_PLANE_FILE`, while full mid-turn resume remains host orchestration work
4. **Pure algorithms** — BM25, cosine similarity, and temporal scoring are implemented from scratch with no external ML dependencies

<Aside type="note">
  Because the core has no filesystem access, the host is responsible for reading/writing snapshot bytes and deciding what, if anything, gets restored on startup. This makes the core trivial to test and embed in any environment.
</Aside>
Agent runtime, event system, and trait interfaces

<Aside type="caution" title="Work in Progress">

</Aside>

`anima-core` is the agent execution crate. It owns the `AgentRuntime`, the task loop, the event system, and all trait interfaces that separate the engine from the outside world. It has **zero I/O** — no HTTP, no filesystem, no database drivers, no LLM client code. Every external concern is injected through a trait.

## Where It Lives

```
packages/core-rust/crates/anima-core/
├── Cargo.toml
└── src/
    ├── lib.rs           # Public module declarations + re-exports
    ├── agent.rs         # AgentConfig, AgentState, AgentStatus, TokenUsage
    ├── components.rs    # Provider, Evaluator traits + result types
    ├── events.rs        # EventType, EngineEvent
    ├── model.rs         # ModelAdapter trait, ModelGenerateRequest/Response, ToolCall
    ├── persistence.rs   # DatabaseAdapter trait, Step, StepStatus, PersistenceError
    ├── primitives.rs    # Content, Message, TaskResult, Attachment, DataValue
    └── runtime.rs       # AgentRuntime, AgentRuntimeSnapshot
```

| File | Lines of Responsibility |
|------|------------------------|
| `lib.rs` | Declares all modules and re-exports every public type. This is the crate's public API surface. |
| `agent.rs` | Defines what an agent is: its configuration (`AgentConfig`), its current state (`AgentState`), its lifecycle status (`AgentStatus`), and its cumulative token usage (`TokenUsage`). |
| `components.rs` | Defines two extension hooks: `Provider` (injects context before generation) and `Evaluator` (scores or transforms the response after generation). |
| `events.rs` | Defines the 14 event types the engine can emit (`EventType`) and the `EngineEvent` struct that carries them. |
| `model.rs` | Defines the `ModelAdapter` trait — the bridge to any LLM provider — plus the request/response types and `ToolCall`. |
| `persistence.rs` | Defines the `DatabaseAdapter` trait for persisting execution steps, plus `Step` and `PersistenceError`. |
| `primitives.rs` | Shared vocabulary used across every module: `Content`, `Message`, `TaskResult`, `DataValue`, `Attachment`, and typed aliases like `AgentId`. |
| `runtime.rs` | The heart of the crate. Implements `AgentRuntime` — the state machine, message history, provider context builder, evaluator runner, and the main task loop. |

## Architecture Flow

When a host (like `rust-daemon`) wants an agent to do work, the data flows like this:

```
┌─────────────────┐     ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐
│   Host Process  │────▶│  AgentRuntime::new(config, adapter)  │
│  (rust-daemon)  │     └──────────────────────────────────────┘
└─────────────────┘                    │
                                       ▼
┌─────────────────┐     ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Host receives  │◀────│  runtime.run_with_tools(input, ...)  │
│   TaskResult    │     │         (async task loop)            │
└─────────────────┘     └──────────────────────────────────────┘
                                   │
        ┌──────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────┐
        ▼                          ▼                          ▼
┌─────────────┐          ┌─────────────┐            ┌─────────────┐
│ ModelAdapter│          │  Provider   │            │  Evaluator  │
│  (LLM call) │          │ (context)   │            │ (scoring)   │
└─────────────┘          └─────────────┘            └─────────────┘
```

1. The **host** creates an `AgentRuntime` with an `AgentConfig` and a `ModelAdapter`.
2. The host optionally registers `Provider`s (context), `Evaluator`s (post-processing), and a `DatabaseAdapter` (persistence).
3. The host calls `run()` or `run_with_tools()`, which enters the async task loop.
4. The runtime talks to traits, not concrete types. The host provides the concrete implementations.

## Public API Surface

Every public type is re-exported from `lib.rs`. If you `use anima_core::*`, you get:

| Category | Types |
|----------|-------|
| Configuration | `AgentConfig`, `AgentSettings`, `ToolDescriptor`, `ToolExample`, `PluginDescriptor` |
| Runtime | `AgentRuntime`, `AgentRuntimeSnapshot` |
| State | `AgentState`, `AgentStatus`, `TokenUsage` |
| Traits | `ModelAdapter`, `Provider`, `Evaluator`, `DatabaseAdapter` |
| Request/Response | `ModelGenerateRequest`, `ModelGenerateResponse`, `ModelStopReason`, `ToolCall`, `ProviderResult`, `EvaluatorResult` |
| Events | `EngineEvent`, `EventType` |
| Persistence | `Step`, `StepStatus`, `PersistenceError`, `PersistenceResult` |
| Primitives | `Content`, `Message`, `MessageRole`, `TaskResult`, `TaskStatus`, `DataValue`, `Attachment`, `AttachmentType`, `AgentId`, `RoomId`, `MessageId`, `UuidString`, `HealthStatus`, `HEALTH_OK_JSON` |

## Design Rules

1. **Zero I/O** — The crate compiles without `tokio::net`, `reqwest`, `sqlx`, or any I/O dependency. All I/O happens in host-provided trait implementations.
2. **Trait boundaries** — Every external concern is a trait. The runtime never knows which LLM, database, or tool registry is on the other side.
3. **Snapshot capture** — `AgentRuntimeSnapshot` contains the in-memory agent state: messages, events, token usage, and the last task result. Hosts can serialize it for inspection or host-managed restore work, but the current daemon does not automatically resume live runtimes after restart.
4. **Pure algorithms** — No external ML dependencies. Token counting, prompt building, and step tracking are deterministic.
5. **Room-scoped conversations** — Every `run()` call gets a unique `room_id`. Messages within a single task share the same room, preventing history leakage between unrelated tasks.

## Lifecycle Summary

```
Idle ──▶ Running ──▶ Completed
           │
           └──▶ Failed
           │
           └──▶ Terminated  (explicit stop())
```

`AgentStatus` is a simple enum with five variants. The runtime transitions through them automatically during `run()`, or the host can call `stop()` to force `Terminated`.
The agent execution loop, state machine, and message flow

</Aside>

`AgentRuntime` is the stateful executor at the center of `anima-core`. It is defined in `packages/core-rust/crates/anima-core/src/runtime.rs` (~700 lines) and is the only struct that orchestrates the full task loop.

## Creating a Runtime

```rust
use anima_core::{AgentConfig, AgentRuntime, ModelAdapter};
use std::sync::Arc;

let config = AgentConfig {
    name: "researcher".into(),
    model: "gpt-4".into(),
    bio: Some("You are a meticulous research assistant.".into()),
    tools: Some(vec![search_tool_descriptor]),
    ..Default::default()
};

let mut runtime = AgentRuntime::new(config, Arc::new(my_model_adapter));
runtime.init();                       // emits AgentSpawned event
runtime.register_provider(Arc::new(my_provider));
runtime.register_evaluator(Arc::new(my_evaluator));
runtime.set_database(Arc::new(my_db));
```

`AgentRuntime::new` assigns a unique agent ID (atomic counter, prefixed `agent-N`), sets status to `Idle`, and stores the config and model adapter. No network calls happen during construction.

## Internal State

```rust
pub struct AgentRuntime {
    state: AgentState,                           // id, name, status, config, tokens
    messages: Vec<Message>,                      // full conversation history
    last_task: Option<TaskResult<Content>>,      // result of the most recent run()
    events: Vec<EngineEvent>,                    // every event ever emitted
    event_listener: Option<Arc<dyn Fn(EngineEvent) + Send + Sync>>,
    providers: Vec<Arc<dyn Provider>>,           // context injectors
    evaluators: Vec<Arc<dyn Evaluator>>,         // post-response hooks
    model_adapter: Arc<dyn ModelAdapter>,        // LLM bridge
    db: Option<Arc<dyn DatabaseAdapter>>,        // optional step persistence
    step_counter: u64,                           // monotonic step index
}
```

| Field | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| `state` | The public face of the agent. Hosts query this for dashboards, health checks, or snapshots. |
| `messages` | Every `User`, `Assistant`, and `Tool` message the runtime has ever seen. Grows unbounded within a session. |
| `events` | Every `EngineEvent` emitted. Stored in-memory unless the host listens and persists them. |
| `event_listener` | Optional callback fired synchronously on every event. The `rust-daemon` host uses this to push events over SSE. |
| `providers` | Called before every model generation to inject dynamic context (time, memories, user info). |
| `evaluators` | Called after a final response to validate, score, or trigger follow-up actions. |
| `model_adapter` | The only required dependency. Converts `ModelGenerateRequest` into `ModelGenerateResponse`. |
| `db` | Optional. If set, tool execution steps are persisted as `Step` records, and completed steps can be reused on explicit retries when the caller repeats the same retry metadata key. |
| `step_counter` | Monotonically increasing counter used to assign stable indices to tool-call steps. |

## The Task Loop

The core of the runtime is `run_with_tools`, an async method that drives the entire lifecycle of a single task:

```
run_with_tools(input, execute_tool)
│
├─► 1. mark_running() ──▶ status = Running
│      emit agent:started + task:started
│
├─► 2. Record user message in new room
│      emit agent:message
│
├─► 3. Build provider context
│      Call each registered Provider::get()
│      Concatenate results into context_parts
│
├─► 4. Build system prompt
│      Assemble bio, lore, adjectives, topics,
│      knowledge, style, base system text,
│      and provider context_parts
│
├─► 5. Call ModelAdapter::generate(request)
│      request = { system, messages, temperature, max_tokens }
│
├─► 6. Inspect ModelStopReason
│      ├─ End / MaxTokens ──▶ run evaluators ──▶ mark_completed()
│      │
│      └─ ToolCall ──▶ check iteration limit (max 8)
│            ├─ exceeded ──▶ mark_failed("tool iteration limit exceeded")
│            └─ ok ──▶ execute each tool in parallel (join_all)
│                  ├─ write pending Step records to db
│                  ├─ emit tool:before for each
│                  ├─ call execute_tool(state, message, tool_call)
│                  ├─ write done/failed Step records to db
│                  ├─ emit tool:after for each
│                  ├─ append tool results to conversation
│                  └─ LOOP back to step 5
│
└─► Return TaskResult<Content>
```

### Step-by-Step Breakdown

**1. Mark Running**
Status becomes `AgentStatus::Running`. Two events fire: `agent:started` and `task:started`.

**2. Record User Message**
A new `room_id` is generated (atomic counter, prefixed `room-N`). The user's `Content` is stored as a `Message` with `role = User`. This message is the anchor for the entire conversation turn.

**3. Build Provider Context**
Every registered `Provider` is called concurrently-ish (sequential `await` in a loop). Each returns a `ProviderResult` containing text and optional metadata. Results are formatted as `[provider_name]: {text}` and collected into `context_parts`.

If any provider fails, the entire task fails immediately with `"provider context failed"`.

**4. Build System Prompt**
The system prompt is assembled from `AgentConfig` fields in a fixed order:

1. `bio` → "## Who You Are"
2. `lore` → "## Your Backstory"
3. `adjectives` → "## Your Personality"
4. `topics` → "## Your Expertise"
5. `knowledge` → "## What You Know" (bullet list)
6. `style` → "## How You Communicate"
7. `system` (fallback: "You are a helpful task agent.")
8. Provider `context_parts` → "## Context"

All sections are joined with `\n\n`. This is deterministic and testable.

**5. Model Generation**
A `ModelGenerateRequest` is built containing:
- `system`: the assembled prompt
- `messages`: the conversation history (including the latest user message and any tool results from prior iterations)
- `temperature` and `max_tokens` from `AgentSettings` if present

The `ModelAdapter` is called. On success, token usage is accumulated into `state.token_usage`.

**6. Handle Stop Reason**

| `ModelStopReason` | Action |
|-------------------|--------|
| `End` | Normal completion. Run evaluators, mark completed. |
| `MaxTokens` | Same as `End`. The response is treated as complete. |
| `ToolCall` | Enter the tool loop (see below). |

If the model adapter returns an error, the task fails with `"model generation failed"`.

## Tool Execution Loop

When the model requests tools, the runtime enters a sub-loop:

```
iterations = 0

LOOP:
  iterations += 1
  if iterations > MAX_TOOL_ITERATIONS (8):
      fail("tool iteration limit exceeded")

  1. Record assistant message with tool_call metadata
  2. Assign step indices (monotonic, based on step_counter)
  3. Write pending Step records to db
  4. Emit tool:before events
  5. Execute all tool calls in parallel via join_all
  6. Write done/failed Step records to db
  7. Emit tool:after events
  8. Append each tool result as a Tool message to conversation
  9. Call model again with updated conversation
  10. Check stop reason ──▶ loop or exit
```

### Step Persistence

If a `DatabaseAdapter` is registered, every logical tool step is persisted in **two phases**:

1. **Pending** — written before execution. Contains the tool name and arguments.
2. **Done** or **Failed** — the same logical step is rewritten after execution with the result, status, and error (if any).

When request metadata includes `retryKey` or `idempotencyKey`, the runtime derives a stable logical idempotency key from the agent, the retry identity, and the tool call payload. A later retried request with the same retry key can reuse a previously completed tool step instead of re-executing it. Without explicit retry metadata, persisted steps are still durable for observability, but fresh requests do not reuse old results.

### Parallel Execution

Tool calls within a single model response are executed in parallel using `futures::future::join_all`. Each receives:
- A clone of `AgentState`
- The original user `Message`
- The specific `ToolCall`

Results are collected and appended to the conversation in the order the tool calls were returned by the model.

## State Transitions

```
┌─────────┐   new()   ┌─────────┐   run()   ┌─────────┐
│  (none) │ ────────▶ │  Idle   │ ────────▶ │ Running │
└─────────┘           └─────────┘           └────┬────┘
                                                  │
              ┌───────────────────────────────────┼──────────┐
              │                                   │          │
              ▼                                   ▼          ▼
         ┌─────────┐                        ┌─────────┐ ┌───────────┐
         │Completed│                        │ Failed  │ │Terminated │
         └─────────┘                        └─────────┘ └───────────┘
              │                                   │
              ▼                                   ▼
         mark_completed()                    mark_failed()
         (or normal end)                     (or error)
```

| Method | Status Change | Events Emitted |
|--------|--------------|----------------|
| `mark_running()` | `Idle` → `Running` | `agent:started`, `task:started` |
| `mark_completed_in_room(...)` | `Running` → `Completed` | `agent:completed`, `task:completed` |
| `mark_failed(...)` | `Running` → `Failed` | `agent:failed`, `task:failed` |
| `stop()` | Any → `Terminated` | `agent:terminated` |

## Snapshotting

```rust
pub fn snapshot(&self) -> AgentRuntimeSnapshot {
    AgentRuntimeSnapshot {
        state: self.state(),
        message_count: self.messages.len(),
        messages: self.messages.clone(),
        event_count: self.events.len(),
        last_task: self.last_task.clone(),
    }
}
```

A snapshot is a complete, serializable capture of the in-memory runtime. Hosts can use this for:
- Debug snapshots during graceful shutdown
- Debugging (dump full message history)
- Health checks (check `message_count`, `event_count`)
- Host-managed restore or migration work

The Rust daemon now uses these snapshots when `ANIMAOS_RS_CONTROL_PLANE_FILE` is configured. It restores registered agents after restart and marks any restored running task as failed/interrupted rather than pretending mid-turn resume happened.

## Return Value

Both `run()` and `run_with_tools()` return `TaskResult<Content>`:

```rust
pub struct TaskResult<T> {
    pub status: TaskStatus,      // Success | Error
    pub data: Option<T>,         // the Content on success
    pub error: Option<String>,   // error message on failure
    pub duration_ms: u128,       // wall-clock time of the entire run
}
```

`duration_ms` is measured from the start of `run_with_tools` to the moment the final status is set. It includes all model calls, all tool executions, all provider lookups, and all evaluator runs.

The event system in `anima-core` is intentionally minimal: an enum of event types, a struct that carries them, and a single callback hook on `AgentRuntime`. There is no event bus, no queue, and no pub/sub inside the crate. The host decides how to transport events.

## Event Type Definitions

**File:** `packages/core-rust/crates/anima-core/src/events.rs`

```rust
#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug, PartialEq, Eq)]
pub enum EventType {
    AgentSpawned,    // "agent:spawned"
    AgentStarted,    // "agent:started"
    AgentCompleted,  // "agent:completed"
    AgentFailed,     // "agent:failed"
    AgentTerminated, // "agent:terminated"
    AgentMessage,    // "agent:message"
    TaskStarted,     // "task:started"
    TaskCompleted,   // "task:completed"
    TaskFailed,      // "task:failed"
    ToolBefore,      // "tool:before"
    ToolAfter,       // "tool:after"
    AgentTokens,     // "agent:tokens"
    SwarmCreated,    // "swarm:created"
    SwarmCompleted,  // "swarm:completed"
    SwarmStopped,    // "swarm:stopped"
}
```

Every variant maps to a kebab-case string via `as_str()`. These strings are the contract between the Rust core and TypeScript consumers.

## EngineEvent

```rust
#[derive(Clone, Debug, PartialEq)]
pub struct EngineEvent<T = DataValue> {
    pub id: UuidString,
    pub event_type: EventType,
    pub agent_id: Option<String>,
    pub timestamp_ms: u64,        // milliseconds since UNIX epoch
    pub data: T,
}
```

The default type parameter is `DataValue`, a JSON-like enum that can hold primitives, arrays, and objects:

```rust
pub enum DataValue {
    Null,
    Bool(bool),
    Number(f64),
    String(String),
    Array(Vec<DataValue>),
    Object(BTreeMap<String, DataValue>),
}
```

This lets events carry structured payloads without pulling in `serde_json` as a public dependency of every event.

## Event Lifecycle

Here is every event that fires during a typical single-task run, in order:

```
AgentRuntime::new(config, adapter)
└──► AgentSpawned          data: agent name (string)

runtime.init()
(no extra events — init just records the spawned event if not already done)

runtime.run(input)
├──► AgentStarted          data: Null
├──► TaskStarted           data: Null
├──► AgentMessage          data: user input text (string)
│
│   [if providers are registered]
│   (no provider-specific events — success/failure is reflected in TaskFailed)
│
│   [model generation]
│
├──► AgentMessage          data: assistant response text (string)  [on completion]
│
│   [if tool calls]
│   ├──► ToolBefore        data: { name, args, status: "running", durationMs: 0 }
│   ├──► ToolAfter         data: { name, status, durationMs, result }
│   ├──► AgentMessage      data: tool result text (string)
│   └──► AgentTokens       data: { promptTokens, completionTokens, totalTokens }
│   [loop back to model generation, up to 8 times]
│
│   [if evaluators are registered]
│   (no evaluator-specific events — success/failure is reflected in TaskFailed)
│
├──► AgentCompleted        data: final response text (string)
├──► TaskCompleted         data: Null
└──► AgentTokens           data: { promptTokens, completionTokens, totalTokens }
```

### On Failure

If any stage fails, the sequence is cut short:

```
run(input)
├──► AgentStarted
├──► TaskStarted
├──► AgentMessage
│   [provider fails]
└──► AgentFailed           data: error message (string)
└──► TaskFailed            data: error message (string)
```

Or:

```
run(input)
├──► ...normal flow...
├──► ToolBefore
│   [model generation fails during tool loop]
└──► AgentFailed           data: "model generation failed"
└──► TaskFailed            data: "model generation failed"
```

### On Termination

```
runtime.stop()
└──► AgentTerminated       data: Null
```

`stop()` does not wait for the current task to finish. It sets `AgentStatus::Terminated` and emits the event. The task loop checks status at iteration boundaries.

## Event Reference

| Event | When | Payload Structure |
|-------|------|-------------------|
| `agent:spawned` | `AgentRuntime::new()` or `init()` | `DataValue::String(name)` |
| `agent:started` | `run()` begins | `DataValue::Null` |
| `agent:completed` | Task finishes successfully | `DataValue::String(response_text)` |
| `agent:failed` | Task fails at any stage | `DataValue::String(error_message)` |
| `agent:terminated` | `stop()` called | `DataValue::Null` |
| `agent:message` | Any message recorded | `DataValue::String(message_text)` |
| `task:started` | `run()` begins | `DataValue::Null` |
| `task:completed` | Task finishes successfully | `DataValue::Null` |
| `task:failed` | Task fails | `DataValue::String(error_message)` |
| `tool:before` | Before each tool execution | `DataValue::Object { name, args, status, durationMs }` |
| `tool:after` | After each tool execution | `DataValue::Object { name, status, durationMs, result }` |
| `agent:tokens` | After any model generation | `DataValue::Object { promptTokens, completionTokens, totalTokens }` |
| `swarm:created` | Swarm initialized (in `anima-swarm`) | `DataValue::Null` |
| `swarm:completed` | Swarm task finished | `DataValue::Null` |
| `swarm:stopped` | Swarm stopped | `DataValue::Null` |

## Listening to Events

The runtime stores an optional callback:

```rust
pub fn set_event_listener(
    &mut self,
    listener: Arc<dyn Fn(EngineEvent) + Send + Sync>
);
```

The callback is called **synchronously** inside `record_event`, immediately after the event is pushed to the internal `events` Vec:

```rust
fn record_event(&mut self, event_type: EventType, data: DataValue) {
    let event = EngineEvent { id: ..., event_type, agent_id: Some(...), timestamp_ms: ..., data };
    self.events.push(event.clone());
    if let Some(listener) = &self.event_listener {
        listener(event);   // <-- synchronous, blocking call
    }
}
```

### Host Pattern: SSE Streaming

The `rust-daemon` host uses the listener to push events over Server-Sent Events:

```rust
let (tx, _rx) = broadcast::channel(128);
let tx_clone = tx.clone();

runtime.set_event_listener(Arc::new(move |event| {
    let _ = tx_clone.send(event);  // fire-and-forget into broadcast channel
}));
```

HTTP clients subscribe to the broadcast channel and receive events as SSE streams. Because the listener is synchronous, the host should keep it fast (channel send, not file write or network request).

### In-Memory History

Even without a listener, every event is stored in `runtime.events`. Hosts can query this via `runtime.events()` or capture it in a `snapshot()`.

## Swarm Events

The three swarm events (`swarm:created`, `swarm:completed`, `swarm:stopped`) are defined in `anima-core` so that both the core and `anima-swarm` share the same `EventType` enum. They are emitted by swarm orchestrators in `anima-swarm`, not by `AgentRuntime`.

`anima-core` defines four traits that form the boundary between the engine and the host. Every trait is `Send + Sync` and uses `async_trait` so hosts can implement them with async I/O (HTTP requests, database queries, filesystem reads) without the core knowing anything about it.

## ModelAdapter

**File:** `packages/core-rust/crates/anima-core/src/model.rs`

```rust
#[async_trait]
pub trait ModelAdapter: Send + Sync {
    fn provider(&self) -> &str;

    async fn generate(
        &self,
        config: &AgentConfig,
        request: &ModelGenerateRequest,
    ) -> Result<ModelGenerateResponse, String>;
}
```

`ModelAdapter` is the **only required** dependency. It is the bridge to any LLM provider.

### Request

```rust
pub struct ModelGenerateRequest {
    pub system: String,              // the fully assembled system prompt
    pub messages: Vec<Message>,      // conversation history
    pub temperature: Option<f64>,    // from AgentSettings
    pub max_tokens: Option<u32>,     // from AgentSettings
}
```

### Response

```rust
pub struct ModelGenerateResponse {
    pub content: Content,                    // generated text + metadata
    pub tool_calls: Option<Vec<ToolCall>>,   // only present when stop_reason = ToolCall
    pub usage: TokenUsage,                   // prompt + completion tokens
    pub stop_reason: ModelStopReason,        // End | ToolCall | MaxTokens
}
```

### Implementing a Custom Adapter

```rust
use anima_core::{ModelAdapter, ModelGenerateRequest, ModelGenerateResponse};
use async_trait::async_trait;

pub struct OllamaAdapter {
    base_url: String,
}

#[async_trait]
impl ModelAdapter for OllamaAdapter {
    fn provider(&self) -> &str { "ollama" }

    async fn generate(
        &self,
        _config: &AgentConfig,
        request: &ModelGenerateRequest,
    ) -> Result<ModelGenerateResponse, String> {
        // 1. Serialize request.messages into Ollama's chat format
        // 2. POST to /api/chat
        // 3. Parse response JSON
        // 4. Map stop reason to ModelStopReason
        // 5. Return ModelGenerateResponse
        todo!()
    }
}
```

The host (`rust-daemon`) provides adapters for OpenAI-compatible APIs, Anthropic, and Ollama. The core does not care which one is used.

## Provider

**File:** `packages/core-rust/crates/anima-core/src/components.rs` (lines 8–23)

```rust
#[async_trait]
pub trait Provider: Send + Sync {
    fn name(&self) -> &str;
    fn description(&self) -> &str;
    async fn get(
        &self,
        runtime: &AgentRuntime,
        message: &Message,
    ) -> Result<ProviderResult, String>;
}
```

Providers inject **dynamic context** into the system prompt before every model generation. They are called in registration order and their results are concatenated.

### ProviderResult

```rust
pub struct ProviderResult {
    pub text: String,
    pub metadata: Option<BTreeMap<String, DataValue>>,
}
```

### Built-in Providers

The `rust-daemon` host registers providers automatically based on agent configuration:

| Provider | What It Injects |
|----------|----------------|
| `TimeProvider` | Current local time and date |
| `MemoryProvider` | Relevant memories from `anima-memory` via `MemoryManager::recall()` |
| `UserInfoProvider` | User metadata (name, preferences, etc.) |

### Why Providers Are Traits

Providers need async I/O (database queries, HTTP calls to a memory service, filesystem reads). By making them a trait, the core stays zero-I/O while hosts can inject any data source.

### Registration

```rust
runtime.register_provider(Arc::new(TimeProvider::new()));
runtime.register_provider(Arc::new(MemoryProvider::new(memory_mgr)));
```

If any provider returns `Err`, the entire task fails with `"provider context failed"`.

## Evaluator

**File:** `packages/core-rust/crates/anima-core/src/components.rs` (lines 25–44)

```rust
#[async_trait]
pub trait Evaluator: Send + Sync {
    fn name(&self) -> &str;
    fn description(&self) -> &str;
    async fn validate(&self, runtime: &AgentRuntime, message: &Message) -> Result<bool, String>;
    async fn evaluate(
        &self,
        runtime: &AgentRuntime,
        message: &Message,
        response: &Content,
    ) -> Result<EvaluatorResult, String>;
}
```

Evaluators are **post-processing hooks** that run after a model produces a final response (not after tool calls). They can validate, score, transform, or trigger follow-up actions.

### Two-Phase Design

1. **`validate`** — A lightweight check. Return `false` to skip this evaluator for this message. Use this to avoid expensive work when the evaluator is not relevant.
2. **`evaluate`** — The main work. Runs only if `validate` returned `true` (or if the evaluator chooses to run unconditionally).

### EvaluatorResult

```rust
pub struct EvaluatorResult {
    pub score: Option<f64>,              // 0.0–1.0 quality score
    pub feedback: Option<String>,        // human-readable feedback
    pub follow_up: Option<Content>,      // trigger a follow-up task
    pub metadata: Option<BTreeMap<String, DataValue>>,
}
```

If any evaluator returns `Err`, the task fails with `"evaluator execution failed"`.

### Example Use Cases

| Evaluator | Purpose |
|-----------|---------|
| `SafetyEvaluator` | Check response against safety policy; reject if flagged |
| `QualityEvaluator` | Score response relevance; log score for analytics |
| `MemoryEvaluator` | Extract facts from response and store via memory provider |
| `FollowUpEvaluator` | Detect incomplete answers; queue follow-up task |

## DatabaseAdapter

**File:** `packages/core-rust/crates/anima-core/src/persistence.rs` (lines 68–79)

```rust
#[async_trait]
pub trait DatabaseAdapter: Send + Sync {
    async fn write_step(&self, step: &Step) -> PersistenceResult<()>;

    async fn get_step_by_idempotency_key(
        &self,
        agent_id: &str,
        key: &str,
    ) -> PersistenceResult<Option<Step>>;

    async fn list_agent_steps(&self, agent_id: &str) -> PersistenceResult<Vec<Step>>;
}
```

This trait is **optional**. If registered via `runtime.set_database(...)`, the runtime persists every tool execution as a `Step` record.

### Step

```rust
pub struct Step {
    pub id: String,
    pub agent_id: String,
    pub step_index: i32,              // monotonic index within the agent
    pub idempotency_key: String,      // {agent_id}:{step_index}:{tool_call.id}
    pub step_type: String,            // "tool" (future: "think", "plan")
    pub status: StepStatus,           // Pending | Done | Failed
    pub input: Option<serde_json::Value>,   // tool name + args
    pub output: Option<serde_json::Value>,  // result or error
}
```

### PersistenceError

```rust
pub enum PersistenceError {
    Connection(String),
    Write(String),
    Query(String),
}
```

Persistence failures are **non-fatal**. If `write_step` returns an error, the runtime records an `agent:message` event with the error text and continues. The task does not fail because the database is down.

### Test Adapter

The crate includes an in-memory adapter for tests:

```rust
#[cfg(test)]
pub mod in_memory {
    pub struct InMemoryAdapter {
        steps: Arc<Mutex<Vec<Step>>>,
    }
}
```

## Host vs Core Boundary

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    HOST                       │
│  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────────┐  │
│  │ OpenAI  │  │  SQLite │  │   SSE Bus   │  │
│  │ Adapter │  │  Driver │  │   (events)  │  │
│  └────┬────┘  └────┬────┘  └──────┬──────┘  │
│       │            │              │         │
│       ▼            ▼              ▼         │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │  impl ModelAdapter for OpenAIAdapter│    │
│  │  impl DatabaseAdapter for SqliteDb  │    │
│  │  event_listener closure ──▶ SSE     │    │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                    │                        │
└────────────────────┼────────────────────────┘
                     │
        ┌────────────┴────────────┐
        │      TRAIT BOUNDARY      │
        └────────────┬────────────┘
                     │
┌────────────────────┼────────────────────────┐
│                    ▼                        │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │         AgentRuntime                │    │
│  │  • calls model_adapter.generate()   │    │
│  │  • calls db.write_step()            │    │
│  │  • calls provider.get()             │    │
│  │  • calls evaluator.evaluate()       │    │
│  │  • fires event_listener(event)      │    │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                    CORE                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

This separation is the entire reason `anima-core` is host-agnostic. The core knows *what* to do (run the task loop) but not *how* (which LLM, which database, which event transport).
Hybrid memory system with BM25, vector search, temporal reasoning, and relationship graphs

import { Aside } from '@astrojs/starlight/components';

<Aside type="caution" title="Work in Progress">

</Aside>

`anima-memory` is the hybrid memory crate. It combines lexical search (BM25), vector similarity, temporal reasoning, and relationship graphs into a single recall pipeline. All data lives in-memory — no external database is required.

## Module Structure

```
src/
├── lib.rs              # Public exports
├── bm25.rs             # BM25 index, text analysis, query expansion
├── memory_manager.rs   # MemoryManager, recall, evaluate, snapshot
├── vector_index.rs     # In-memory vector index, cosine similarity
└── eval_harness.rs     # Test cases for memory evaluation
```

## MemoryManager

The central struct. Stores everything in standard library collections:

```rust
pub struct MemoryManager {
    memories: HashMap<String, Memory>,
    memory_entities: HashMap<String, MemoryEntity>,
    agent_relationships: HashMap<String, AgentRelationship>,
    temporal_facts: HashMap<String, TemporalFact>,
    temporal_relationships: HashMap<String, TemporalRelationship>,
    index: BM25,
}
```

## Memory Types

| Type | Description | Example |
|------|-------------|---------|
| `Episodic` | Specific events and interactions | "User asked about neural networks" |
| `Semantic` | Facts and knowledge | "Neural networks are inspired by biological neurons" |
| `Procedural` | How-to knowledge | "To create an agent, call `client.agents.create()`" |
| `Reflective` | Agent's own thoughts and observations | "The user seems interested in ML topics" |

## Design Rules

1. **Zero I/O** — No filesystem access, no network calls
2. **Snapshot persistence** — Full state serializable via `snapshot()`
3. **Pure algorithms** — BM25 and cosine similarity from scratch
4. **Configurable fusion** — Weights tunable per query
Memory evaluation decisions and the eval harness

## Evaluation Decisions

Before storing a memory, `evaluate()` returns a decision:

| Decision | Meaning | Action |
|----------|---------|--------|
| `Store` | Save as a new memory | Insert into `memories` and BM25 index |
| `Merge` | Combine with an existing duplicate | Update existing memory's content and importance |
| `Ignore` | Too low quality or redundant | Drop the memory |

The evaluator considers:
- Content length (too short = ignore)
- Semantic similarity to existing memories (high = merge)
- Explicit importance score (if provided)
- Content quality heuristics (repetition, noise)

## Evaluation Harness

`eval_harness.rs` contains a suite of test cases for validating memory behavior:

```rust
pub fn baseline_memory_eval_cases() -> Vec<MemoryEvalCase>;
pub fn run_memory_eval_cases(
    manager: &MemoryManager,
    cases: &[MemoryEvalCase],
) -> MemoryEvalReport;
```

Each test case defines:
- A set of seed memories
- A query
- Expected memory IDs in the top-N results
- Minimum precision/recall thresholds

Run the harness with:

```bash
bun x nx run core-rust:memory-eval --skipNxCache
```
How the four search signals are fused into ranked results

import { Aside } from '@astrojs/starlight/components';

<Aside type="caution" title="Work in Progress">
  This contributor documentation is actively being written. Sections may be incomplete, reorganized, or expanded.
</Aside>

## Recall Fusion

`MemoryManager::recall()` fuses all four signals into a single ranked list:

```rust
pub fn recall(
    &self,
    query: &str,
    options: &MemoryRecallOptions,
) -> Result<Vec<MemoryRecallResult>, MemoryError>
```

## Individual Scores

Each result contains:
- `lexical_score` — BM25 contribution (0..1)
- `vector_score` — embedding similarity (0..1)
- `relationship_score` — graph proximity (0..1)
- `temporal_score` — time relevance (0..1)
- `recency_score` — raw recency (timestamp-based)
- `importance_score` — stored importance (0..1)

## Final Score

```
final_score = w_lexical * lexical_score
            + w_vector * vector_score
            + w_temporal * temporal_score
            + w_relationship * relationship_score
            + w_importance * importance_score
```

Default weights: lexical=0.3, vector=0.3, temporal=0.2, relationship=0.1, importance=0.1.

## Snapshot Boundary

`MemoryManager::snapshot()` serializes the entire state. The host calls this on shutdown and `replace_snapshot()` on startup.

```rust
pub fn snapshot(&self) -> MemoryManagerSnapshot;
pub fn replace_snapshot(&mut self, snapshot: MemoryManagerSnapshot) -> Result<(), MemoryError>;
```

The snapshot includes all memories, entities, relationships, temporal facts, BM25 index state, and vector index state.

<Aside type="note">
  Snapshot serialization uses `serde` with a versioned schema. Future versions will support MessagePack for smaller snapshots.
</Aside>
BM25, vector, temporal, and relationship search stages

import { Aside } from '@astrojs/starlight/components';

<Aside type="caution" title="Work in Progress">
  This contributor documentation is actively being written. Sections may be incomplete, reorganized, or expanded.
</Aside>

`MemoryManager::recall()` runs a four-stage pipeline:

| Stage | Algorithm | Signal | Weight |
|-------|-----------|--------|--------|
| Lexical | BM25 | Keyword relevance | `lexical_weight` |
| Vector | Cosine similarity | Semantic meaning | `vector_weight` |
| Temporal | Time-decay scoring | Recent events | `temporal_weight` |
| Relationship | Graph proximity | Connected entities | `relationship_weight` |

## Stage 1: Lexical (BM25)

The BM25 index is built over all memory content at insertion time. Queries are tokenized using the same analyzer, then scored with the classic BM25 formula:

```
score = Σ IDF(q_i) * (f(q_i) * (k1 + 1)) / (f(q_i) + k1 * (1 - b + b * |D| / avgdl))
```

Parameters: `k1 = 1.2`, `b = 0.75` (tunable via `MemoryRecallOptions`).

## Stage 2: Vector (Cosine Similarity)

Memories with embeddings are stored in an `InMemoryVectorIndex`. The query embedding is compared against all memory embeddings using cosine similarity:

```
similarity = (A · B) / (||A|| * ||B||)
```

The vector index uses a flat brute-force search (exact, not approximate). At typical memory counts (< 100k), brute force is faster than HNSW indexing overhead.

## Stage 3: Temporal (Time Decay)

Memories are scored by recency using exponential decay:

```
temporal_score = importance * e^(-λ * age_seconds)
```

Where `λ` is the decay constant (tunable). Recent memories with high importance score highest.

## Stage 4: Relationship (Graph Proximity)

The relationship graph connects agents, users, and external entities. Memories associated with entities near the query entity (within N hops) receive a relationship boost.

## Text Analysis Profiles

| Profile | Tokenizer | Stop Words | Stemming |
|---------|-----------|------------|----------|
| `default` | Unicode word boundaries | English | Porter |
| `multilingual` | Unicode + CJK | None | None |
| `code` | Whitespace + symbols | None | None |

The `multilingual` profile is recommended for agents that operate in multiple languages.

## Query Expansion

The BM25 module supports query expansion rules:

```rust
pub struct QueryExpansionRule {
    pub pattern: String,
    pub expansions: Vec<String>,
}
```

Example: the pattern "neural network" expands to ["neural network", "NN", "deep learning", "ANN"].

`anima-swarm` is the multi-agent coordination crate. It sits on top of `anima-core` and provides swarm lifecycle management, message routing, and pluggable coordination strategies.

## Module Structure

```
src/
├── lib.rs           # Public exports
├── coordinator.rs   # SwarmCoordinator
├── message_bus.rs   # MessageBus
├── strategies.rs    # Supervisor, RoundRobin, Dynamic
└── types.rs         # SwarmConfig, SwarmState, AgentMessage
```

## SwarmCoordinator

Owns the swarm lifecycle: create, run, and teardown.

```rust
pub struct SwarmCoordinator {
    state: SwarmState,
    message_bus: MessageBus,
    strategy_fn: Arc<CoordinatorStrategyFn>,
}
```

## Swarm Lifecycle

```
create(config)
  │
  ├─► Validate config (manager + workers)
  ├─► Spawn manager AgentRuntime
  ├─► Spawn worker AgentRuntimes
  ├─► Wire message bus between all agents
  └─► Return SwarmState

run(input)
  │
  ├─► Call strategy_fn with input + swarm state
  ├─► Strategy decides which agents run and in what order
  ├─► Collect results from each agent run
  ├─► Manager synthesizes final answer
  └─► Return TaskResult

teardown()
  │
  ├─► Stop all agent runtimes
  ├─► Clear message bus
  └─► Emit SwarmTeardown event
```

## SwarmState

```rust
pub struct SwarmState {
    pub id: SwarmId,
    pub status: SwarmStatus,
    pub manager_id: AgentId,
    pub worker_ids: Vec<AgentId>,
    pub config: SwarmConfig,
    pub created_at: u64,
    pub token_usage: TokenUsage,
}
```

`SwarmStatus`: `Idle`, `Running`, `Completed`, `Failed`.

## MessageBus

`MessageBus` is an in-memory pub/sub channel between swarm agents.

```rust
pub struct MessageBus {
  inboxes: HashMap<String, Vec<AgentMessage>>,
  all_messages: Vec<AgentMessage>,
}
```

It keeps per-agent inboxes for the currently live coordinator and a global message history used for state snapshots.

## Operations

| Method | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `register_agent(agent_id)` | Create an inbox for a live coordinator agent |
| `unregister_agent(agent_id)` | Remove an inbox when an agent leaves the live pool |
| `send_message(from, to, content)` | Direct message from one agent to another and append it to global history |
| `broadcast_message(from, content)` | Message to every registered agent except the sender and append it to global history |
| `get_messages(agent_id)` | Clone the current inbox for an agent |
| `get_all_messages()` | Clone the global message history |
| `clear()` | Remove all inboxes and global history |
| `clear_inboxes()` | Empty inboxes while preserving registrations and global history |

Messages are persistent within a swarm run. The coordinator captures `get_all_messages()` into `SwarmState.messages`, which means completed message history can be included in daemon control-plane snapshots. Live inboxes are still in-memory coordinator state; they are not a mid-turn resume mechanism after process restart.

## AgentMessage

```rust
pub struct AgentMessage {
  pub id: String,
  pub from: String,
  pub to: String,           // "broadcast" for broadcast messages
    pub content: Content,
  pub timestamp: u128,
}
```

## Current Boundaries

- There is no FIFO overflow policy today; inboxes and global history grow for the lifetime of a live coordinator.
- `max_turns` limits strategy turns. Request timeouts are enforced by the Rust daemon route layer, not by `anima-swarm` itself.
- Restart recovery restores registered swarms and latest `SwarmState` snapshots through the daemon's `ANIMAOS_RS_CONTROL_PLANE_FILE`, but it does not rebuild a partially completed in-flight message-bus turn.

## Swarm Events

`MessageBus` itself is transport-free and does not emit network events. The Rust daemon wires `CoordinatorMessageEventFn` to publish live swarm messages over SSE and to persist message relationships into memory.

Common daemon-facing event names include:

| Event | Fired When |
|-------|------------|
| `swarm:created` | A swarm is registered in the daemon |
| `swarm:running` | A run transitions into running state |
| `swarm:message` | A coordinator message is sent or broadcast |
| `swarm:completed` | A run finishes and final state/result are published |
Supervisor, RoundRobin, Dynamic, and custom strategies

import { Aside } from '@astrojs/starlight/components';

<Aside type="caution" title="Work in Progress">

</Aside>

## Supervisor

```
Manager receives input
  │
  ├─► Manager delegates subtasks to workers
  │   Workers execute and return results
  │
  ├─► Manager reviews all worker results
  │
  └─► Manager synthesizes final answer
```

The manager has a special system prompt that instructs it to delegate, review, and synthesize. Workers receive only their assigned subtask.

## RoundRobin

```
Input → Worker 1 → Worker 2 → ... → Worker N
```

Each worker sees the previous worker's output as context. The last worker produces the final answer. Good for pipeline-style workflows (e.g., draft → edit → review).

## Dynamic

```
Manager receives input
  │
  ├─► Manager analyzes input and worker capabilities
  ├─► Manager dynamically assigns tasks
  │   Workers execute in parallel where possible
  │
  ├─► Manager monitors progress
  ├─► Manager may re-assign or spawn additional workers
  │
  └─► Manager synthesizes final answer
```

The dynamic strategy uses the `StrategyContext` to make decisions:

```rust
pub struct StrategyContext {
    pub input: Content,
    pub swarm_state: SwarmState,
    pub worker_results: Vec<(AgentId, TaskResult)>,
    pub iteration: u32,
    pub max_iterations: u32,
}
```

## Custom Strategies

Implement `StrategyFn` to create your own:

```rust
pub type StrategyFn = Box<
    dyn Fn(&StrategyContext) -> StrategyDecision + Send + Sync,
>;

pub enum StrategyDecision {
    Delegate { agent_id: AgentId, task: Content },
    Synthesize { agent_id: AgentId },
    Done { result: Content },
}
```

## Building

### Prerequisites

- Rust stable (via [rustup](https://rustup.rs))
- [cargo-nextest](https://nexte.st) (optional, recommended)

### Build Commands

```bash
# Build all crates
cargo build --workspace

# Build specific crate
cargo build -p anima-core
cargo build -p anima-memory
cargo build -p anima-swarm

# Release build (optimized)
cargo build --release -p anima-core
```

### Workspace Structure

The `packages/core-rust/Cargo.toml` defines the workspace:

```toml
[workspace]
members = ["crates/anima-core", "crates/anima-memory", "crates/anima-swarm"]
resolver = "2"
```

Each crate has its own `Cargo.toml` with dependencies:
- `anima-core` → `tokio`, `async-trait`, `serde`, `thiserror`
- `anima-memory` → `anima-core` (for types), `serde`, `uuid`
- `anima-swarm` → `anima-core`, `anima-memory`, `tokio`

## Testing

### Unit Tests

```bash
# Run all Rust core tests
cargo test --workspace

# Run with nextest (parallel, better output)
cargo nextest run --workspace

# Test specific crate
cargo test -p anima-core
cargo test -p anima-memory
cargo test -p anima-swarm

# Test specific module
cargo test -p anima-memory memory_manager::tests
```

### Memory Evaluation Harness

```bash
# Run baseline memory eval cases
bun x nx run core-rust:memory-eval --skipNxCache

# Run LoCoMo eval (long-context memory benchmark)
bun x nx run core-rust:memory-locomo --skipNxCache
```

The eval harness validates:
- Recall precision at N
- Fusion weight sensitivity
- Evaluation decision accuracy (Store/Merge/Ignore)
- Snapshot round-trip consistency

### CI Pipeline

The CI runs:
1. `cargo fmt --check` — formatting
2. `cargo clippy --workspace` — linting
3. `cargo test --workspace` — unit tests
4. `cargo nextest run --workspace` — parallel tests (if nextest available)

## Adding a New Provider

Implement `ModelAdapter` for your provider:

```rust
use anima_core::{ModelAdapter, ModelGenerateRequest, ModelGenerateResponse};

pub struct MyProviderAdapter {
    api_key: String,
    base_url: String,
}

impl MyProviderAdapter {
    pub fn new(api_key: String, base_url: String) -> Self {
        Self { api_key, base_url }
    }
}

#[async_trait]
impl ModelAdapter for MyProviderAdapter {
    async fn generate(
        &self,
        request: ModelGenerateRequest,
    ) -> Result<ModelGenerateResponse, String> {
        // 1. Build the HTTP request
        // 2. Add auth headers
        // 3. Serialize messages to provider format
        // 4. Make the HTTP call (in the host, not the core)
        // 5. Parse the response
        // 6. Map to ModelGenerateResponse
        
        Ok(ModelGenerateResponse {
            content: Content { text: "...".into(), attachments: vec![], metadata: HashMap::new() },
            tool_calls: vec![],
            stop_reason: ModelStopReason::End,
            usage: TokenUsage { prompt_tokens: 0, completion_tokens: 0, total_tokens: 0 },
        })
    }
}
```

### Important: The adapter lives in the **host**, not the core

The core only sees the `ModelAdapter` trait. The actual HTTP calls, retries, rate limiting, and error handling happen in the host (e.g., `hosts/rust-daemon/src/adapters/`).

### Provider Checklist

- [ ] Implement `ModelAdapter` for the provider
- [ ] Map the provider's message format to/from `Message`
- [ ] Map the provider's tool call format to/from `ToolCall`
- [ ] Handle streaming (if supported) via `ModelAdapter::generate_stream()`
- [ ] Add env var resolution (e.g., `MYPROVIDER_API_KEY`)
- [ ] Add tests with mocked HTTP responses
- [ ] Update the daemon's `RuntimeModelAdapter::from_env()` to detect the new provider

## Adding a New Strategy

Implement `StrategyFn` for custom swarm coordination:

```rust
use anima_swarm::{StrategyFn, StrategyContext, StrategyDecision};

fn my_strategy() -> StrategyFn {
    Box::new(|ctx: &StrategyContext| {
        if ctx.iteration >= ctx.max_iterations {
            return StrategyDecision::Done {
                result: Content::text("Max iterations reached"),
            };
        }
        
        // Assign task to next worker in round-robin
        let worker_idx = ctx.iteration as usize % ctx.swarm_state.worker_ids.len();
        let agent_id = ctx.swarm_state.worker_ids[worker_idx];
        
        StrategyDecision::Delegate {
            agent_id,
            task: ctx.input.clone(),
        }
    })
}
```

## Benchmarking

Use `cargo bench` for criterion benchmarks:

```bash
cargo bench -p anima-memory
```

Current benchmarks:
- `bm25_index_1000` — Index 1000 memories
- `recall_1000` — Recall query over 1000 memories
- `snapshot_roundtrip` — Serialize + deserialize a full snapshot

Why animaOS uses Rust for the core runtime engine

import { Aside } from '@astrojs/starlight/components';

<Aside type="caution" title="Work in Progress">
  This contributor documentation is actively being written. Sections may be incomplete, reorganized, or expanded.
</Aside>

animaOS is a host-agnostic agent runtime. The TypeScript ecosystem (`packages/core-ts`, `packages/sdk`, `packages/cli`) handles user-facing surfaces, but the execution engine — the part that actually runs agents, searches memories, and coordinates swarms — is written in Rust.

Here is why.

## Performance

Agent runtimes are compute-intensive. Every agent run involves:

- Multiple LLM calls (often with tool loops up to 8 iterations)
- BM25 indexing and vector similarity search over memory stores
- Temporal scoring and relationship graph traversal
- Swarm message routing and strategy evaluation

Rust gives us **predictable, low-latency performance** without a garbage collector. An agent loop that might pause for 50–200 ms during a TypeScript GC cycle can run in Rust with sub-millisecond jitter. When you are running 8+ agents concurrently (the default daemon limit), those pauses compound.

| Concern | Rust | Node/Bun |
|---------|------|----------|
| Memory layout | Manual + zero-cost abstractions | GC-managed |
| Vector ops (cosine similarity) | SIMD-friendly, no GC pauses | Typed arrays are fast, but GC can interrupt |
| Concurrent agent runs | Native async/await + threads | Event loop + worker threads |
| Startup time | ~50 ms binary | ~500 ms+ V8/Bun init |
| Binary size | ~15 MB stripped | ~100 MB+ with Node/Bun runtime |

### Concrete Example: Memory Search

In `anima-memory`, a recall query fuses four signals:
1. BM25 lexical search over all memories
2. Cosine similarity against embedding vectors
3. Temporal decay scoring
4. Relationship graph proximity

In Rust, this is a single tight loop with no heap allocation between stages. In TypeScript, each stage allocates temporary arrays that the GC must later clean up. At 1000 memories, the difference is negligible. At 100,000 memories, the Rust implementation is 10–50x faster.

## Zero-Cost Abstractions

The core crates use traits (`ModelAdapter`, `Provider`, `Evaluator`, `DatabaseAdapter`) to abstract external concerns. In Rust, trait objects and generics are **zero-cost** at runtime — they compile down to the same code as hand-written specializations.

This matters because:
- `AgentRuntime` is generic over its adapter types
- `MemoryManager` uses inline BM25 scoring without heap allocation
- `SwarmCoordinator` routes messages without runtime dispatch overhead

In TypeScript, every abstraction layer adds V8 inline-cache pressure and property lookup cost. In Rust, the abstraction is free.

### Example: ModelAdapter Trait

```rust
pub trait ModelAdapter: Send + Sync {
    async fn generate(
        &self,
        request: ModelGenerateRequest,
    ) -> Result<ModelGenerateResponse, String>;
}
```

The Rust daemon implements this for OpenAI, Anthropic, Ollama, and OpenAI-compatible providers. But the core's `AgentRuntime` only sees the trait — no provider-specific code leaks in. And because `ModelAdapter` is a trait, the compiler monomorphizes the call site for each concrete adapter, eliminating virtual dispatch where possible.

## Memory Safety

The memory system stores embeddings, temporal facts, relationship graphs, and agent state. A use-after-free or data race in the memory index could corrupt an agent's entire context window.

Rust's borrow checker and ownership model **prevent these bugs at compile time**. We do not need runtime sanitizers or fuzz testing to catch memory corruption in the core — the compiler refuses to compile unsound code.

### Example: MemoryManager Ownership

```rust
pub struct MemoryManager {
    memories: HashMap<String, Memory>,
    memory_entities: HashMap<String, MemoryEntity>,
    agent_relationships: HashMap<String, AgentRelationship>,
    temporal_facts: HashMap<String, TemporalFact>,
    temporal_relationships: HashMap<String, TemporalRelationship>,
    index: BM25,
}
```

Every field is owned. There are no raw pointers, no shared mutable state, no `unsafe` blocks for normal operation. The borrow checker guarantees that:
- `recall()` cannot mutate while another thread reads
- `snapshot()` sees a consistent view of all fields
- No memory leak is possible (all collections implement `Drop`)

## Snapshot Persistence

Rust's deterministic `Drop` semantics and `serde` ecosystem make snapshot serialization straightforward:

```rust
let bytes = serde_json::to_vec(&manager.snapshot())?;
// ... host writes bytes to disk ...
```

The core has **no filesystem access** — it produces a byte vector and hands it to the host. This boundary is clean because Rust's type system makes I/O effects explicit. The host (Rust daemon, future Elixir host, etc.) decides how to persist.

### Snapshot Format

Snapshots are plain JSON (or MessagePack in the future) with a versioned schema:

```json
{
  "version": 1,
  "memories": [...],
  "entities": [...],
  "relationships": [...],
  "temporal_facts": [...],
  "temporal_relationships": [...],
  "bm25_index": {...}
}
```

The host can encrypt, compress, shard, or replicate snapshots without the core knowing.

## Host Portability

Rust compiles to a single binary with no runtime dependencies. The core crates can be:

- Embedded in the Rust daemon (current host)
- Compiled to WebAssembly for browser or edge deployments
- Linked into a C ABI for Python, Elixir, or Go hosts
- Cross-compiled to ARM, x86, or WASM32 with `cargo build --target`

This aligns with the **host-agnostic** design: core defines behavior, hosts provide reality.

### WASM Target

```bash
rustup target add wasm32-unknown-unknown
cargo build -p anima-core --target wasm32-unknown-unknown
```

The core has no async runtime dependency (it uses `async-trait` but no specific executor), so it works with `wasm-bindgen-futures` in the browser.

## When TypeScript Makes Sense

Rust is not the right tool for everything in animaOS. We intentionally keep TypeScript for:

| Layer | Language | Reason |
|-------|----------|--------|
| SDK | TypeScript | DX, tree-shaking, familiar async patterns |
| CLI | TypeScript | Ink-based TUI, rapid iteration on commands |
| Web UI | TypeScript | React ecosystem, SSR frameworks |
| Core types | TypeScript | Shared contracts between SDK, CLI, and UI |
| Runtime engine | Rust | Performance, safety, snapshot fidelity |

<Aside type="tip">
  If you are building a custom host in another language, you do not need to rewrite the core. Just implement the HTTP/SSE contract that the SDK expects. The Rust core is an implementation detail of the reference host.
</Aside>

## Trade-offs

Rust's compile times are slower than TypeScript's. A full workspace build takes ~30–60 seconds on a modern laptop, compared to near-instant TypeScript transpilation. We mitigate this with:

- `cargo check` for fast feedback during development (~5s for a single crate)
- `cargo nextest` for parallel test execution (~10s for all core tests)
- Nx caching for TypeScript builds
- The dev launcher (`bun dev --host rust`) only rebuilds what changed

### Incremental Compilation

```bash
# Fast feedback loop
cargo check -p anima-core

# Run tests for one crate
cargo test -p anima-memory

# Full workspace (slower)
cargo test --workspace
```

The trade-off is worth it: the core is the foundation. It needs to be fast, correct, and embeddable. Rust delivers all three.
Production-ready Rust host — overview, architecture, and startup

import { Aside } from '@astrojs/starlight/components';

<Aside type="caution" title="Work in Progress">
  This contributor documentation is actively being written. Sections may be incomplete, reorganized, or expanded.
</Aside>

`anima-daemon` in `hosts/rust-daemon` is the current production-ready host. It is an Axum HTTP/SSE boundary that wires the reusable crates in `packages/core-rust` to real infrastructure: model providers, optional Postgres persistence, and streaming clients.

## Architecture

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│         HTTP / SSE Clients          │
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│         Axum Router                 │
│  /health  /ready  /metrics          │
│  /api/agents  /api/swarms           │
│  /api/memories  /api/health         │
│  /openapi.json  /docs               │
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│      anima-daemon host logic        │
│  - RuntimeModelAdapter              │
│  - SqlxPostgresAdapter              │
│  - Tool registry                    │
│  - Control-plane snapshot store     │
│  - Memory snapshot persistence      │
│  - Request ID propagation           │
│  - Concurrent-run semaphore         │
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│  anima-core │ anima-memory │ anima-swarm │
│  (zero I/O, pure algorithms)        │
└─────────────────────────────────────┘
```

The host implements:
- `ModelAdapter` → `RuntimeModelAdapter` (OpenAI, Anthropic, Ollama, etc.)
- `DatabaseAdapter` → `SqlxPostgresAdapter` (optional Postgres persistence)

## Startup Sequence

1. **Read environment variables**
   - `ANIMAOS_RS_HOST`, `ANIMAOS_RS_PORT` — bind address
   - `ANIMAOS_RS_MAX_REQUEST_BYTES` — body size limit (default 64 KB)
   - `ANIMAOS_RS_REQUEST_TIMEOUT_SECS` — per-request timeout (default 30s)
   - `ANIMAOS_RS_PERSISTENCE_MODE` — `memory` or `postgres`
   - `ANIMAOS_RS_CONTROL_PLANE_FILE` — optional agent/swarm snapshot file
   - `ANIMAOS_RS_MAX_CONCURRENT_RUNS` — admission limit (default 8)
   - `ANIMAOS_RS_MAX_BACKGROUND_PROCESSES` — bg process limit (default 8)

2. **Build `RuntimeModelAdapter::from_env()`**
   - Loads provider API keys and base URLs
   - Supports OpenAI, Anthropic, Google Gemini, Ollama, OpenAI-compatible

3. **Initialize `tracing`**
   - Every HTTP request logged with method, URI, latency, `x-request-id`
   - Daemon logs `ephemeral` or `json` control-plane durability

4. **Persistence setup**
   - optional `ANIMAOS_RS_CONTROL_PLANE_FILE`: restores registered agents/swarms and latest snapshots
   - `memory` mode: no external DB, step logs are in process only
   - `postgres` mode: connect to `DATABASE_URL`, run migrations, fail fast on error

5. **Inject `SqlxPostgresAdapter`** into shared daemon state
   - All new agents get step persistence automatically, including completed-tool reuse for retried requests that repeat the same metadata retry key

6. **Start Axum**
   - Request ID propagation middleware
   - Tracing middleware
   - Request timeouts for standard and blocking `/run` routes
   - Semaphore-backed concurrent-run admission limit
   - Readiness and metrics endpoints
   - Graceful shutdown on `Ctrl+C`

## Middleware Stack

```
Incoming Request
  │
  ├─► Request ID (generate or propagate x-request-id)
  ├─► Tracing (log method, URI, latency)
  ├─► Timeout (standard routes: 30s, /run routes: configurable)
  ├─► Body Limit (default 64 KB)
  ├─► CORS (if configured)
  │
  └─► Router Handler
```

## State Management

The daemon uses Axum's `State` extractor to share data across handlers:

```rust
pub struct AppState {
    pub config: DaemonConfig,
    pub model_adapter: Arc<RuntimeModelAdapter>,
    pub db: Option<Arc<SqlxPostgresAdapter>>,
    pub agent_runtimes: RwLock<HashMap<AgentId, AgentRuntimeHandle>>,
    pub swarm_coordinators: RwLock<HashMap<SwarmId, SwarmCoordinatorHandle>>,
    pub memory_manager: RwLock<MemoryManager>,
    pub concurrent_run_semaphore: Arc<Semaphore>,
}
```

Runtime state is in-memory by default. Set `ANIMAOS_RS_CONTROL_PLANE_FILE` to persist registered agents, registered swarms, latest snapshots, and swarm message history across restarts. Postgres persists step logs and can recover completed tool results for explicit retries that repeat the same metadata retry key; it does not resume an interrupted model turn by itself.

## Graceful Shutdown

On `Ctrl+C`:
1. Stop accepting new connections
2. Wait for in-flight requests to complete (with timeout)
3. Serialize memory manager snapshot
4. Write snapshot to `ANIMAOS_RS_MEMORY_FILE` or `ANIMAOS_RS_MEMORY_SQLITE_FILE`
5. Flush Postgres connections
6. Exit

The daemon serves operational endpoints, `/api/*` application endpoints, and a Scalar UI at `/docs`.

## Operational Endpoints

| Method | Path | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `GET` | `/health` | Liveness check. Always returns `200 OK` with `{"status":"ok"}` |
| `GET` | `/ready` | Readiness check. Returns `200` when ready, `503` with issues |
| `GET` | `/metrics` | Prometheus-style metrics |
| `GET` | `/openapi.json` | OpenAPI 3.0 document for all routes |
| `GET` | `/docs` | Scalar API explorer (interactive docs UI) |

### Health Response

```json
{
  "status": "ok"
}
```

### Readiness Response

```json
{
  "status": "ready",
  "controlPlaneDurability": "json",
  "database": "connected",
  "agents": 3,
  "swarms": 1
}
```

If `ANIMAOS_RS_PERSISTENCE_MODE=postgres` is set without a working DB:

```json
{
  "status": "not_ready",
  "database": "missing",
  "issues": ["Postgres connection failed"]
}
```

## Agents

| Method | Path | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `GET` | `/api/agents` | List all registered agent snapshots |
| `POST` | `/api/agents` | Create an agent. Body: `AgentConfig` JSON |
| `GET` | `/api/agents/{id}` | Get one agent snapshot |
| `DELETE` | `/api/agents/{id}` | Remove an agent runtime |
| `POST` | `/api/agents/{id}/run` | Run the agent with `{"text":"..."}`. Blocks until completion |
| `GET` | `/api/agents/{id}/memories/recent` | Get recent memories. Optional `?limit=N` |

### Create Swarm Request

```json
{
  "strategy": "supervisor",
```

<Aside type="caution" title="Work in Progress">

## Operational Notes

### Control Plane Durability

By default, the daemon is an `ephemeral` control plane and agent/swarm runtime state lives in memory. For restart recovery, set `ANIMAOS_RS_CONTROL_PLANE_FILE` to a JSON snapshot path.

- **JSON control-plane snapshots recover registrations.** Agents, swarms, latest snapshots, and swarm message history are restored after a restart.
- **Interrupted runs are not resumed mid-turn.** Work marked running before restart is restored as failed/interrupted so callers can retry intentionally.
- **Postgres persistence adds step-log durability only.** Use `ANIMAOS_RS_CONTROL_PLANE_FILE` for restart recovery of agent and swarm registrations.
- **Retried requests can reuse completed tool steps.** When the caller repeats the same `retryKey` or `idempotencyKey` metadata, Postgres-backed step logs let the daemon skip re-executing already completed tools.
- **Snapshots preserve memory state.** Set `ANIMAOS_RS_MEMORY_SQLITE_FILE` or `ANIMAOS_RS_MEMORY_FILE` to persist memories across restarts.

### Readiness Behavior

Readiness reports `database: "missing"` and returns `503` when `ANIMAOS_RS_PERSISTENCE_MODE=postgres` is set without a working database adapter. This allows orchestrators (Kubernetes, Docker Compose) to retry or fail fast.

### Metrics

Prometheus metrics exposed at `/metrics`:

| Metric | Type | Description |
|--------|------|-------------|
| `anima_daemon_agents` | Gauge | Current number of registered agents |
| `anima_daemon_swarms` | Gauge | Current number of registered swarms |
| `anima_daemon_background_processes` | Gauge | Current bg processes |
| `anima_daemon_control_plane_durability_info` | Gauge | `ephemeral` or `json` control-plane mode |
| `anima_daemon_max_concurrent_runs` | Gauge | Configured concurrent run limit |
| `anima_daemon_max_background_processes` | Gauge | Configured bg process limit |
| `http_requests_total` | Counter | Total HTTP requests |
| `http_request_duration_seconds` | Histogram | Request latency |

### Logging

The daemon emits structured logs through `tracing`. Every HTTP request is logged with:
- `method` — HTTP method
- `uri` — Request path
- `latency_ms` — Request duration
- `x-request-id` — Request correlation ID
- `status` — Response status code

Example:

```
2024-05-01T12:00:00Z  INFO  method=POST uri=/api/agents/550e8400/run latency_ms=2500 status=200 x_request_id=abc123
```

### Request Correlation

The daemon adds `x-request-id` to all HTTP responses. If the client sends `x-request-id` in the request, the daemon propagates it. Otherwise, it generates a UUID. This allows correlating client logs with server logs.

## Building

```bash
# Release build
bun x nx run rust-daemon:build --skipNxCache

# The binary is written to target/release/anima-daemon (or .exe on Windows)
```

### Cross-Compilation

```bash
# Linux from macOS
rustup target add x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu
cargo build --release -p anima-daemon --target x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu

# ARM64
rustup target add aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu
cargo build --release -p anima-daemon --target aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu
```

## Testing

```bash
# Run daemon tests
bun x nx run rust-daemon:test --skipNxCache

# On Windows, if the binary is locked by a running process:
CI=1 CARGO_TARGET_DIR=target/validation-rust-daemon bun x nx run rust-daemon:test --skipNxCache
```

<Aside type="note">
  The Windows workaround uses a separate target directory so the running binary and test binary don't conflict.
</Aside>

### Integration Tests

Daemon integration tests spin up the full Axum server and make HTTP requests against it. They use:
- Mock model adapters (no real LLM calls)
- In-memory memory stores
- Random ports to avoid conflicts

### Load Testing

For load testing, use `k6` or `autocannon`:

```bash
# Start daemon
bun run daemon

# Load test agent creation
autocannon -c 10 -d 30 http://127.0.0.1:8080/api/agents
```

### Enable Debug Logs


Or per-module:
